FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective analysis of U.S. ABCD Study (5.0 release), 21 sites, participants aged 11-12 at Year 2 (2018-2020), N=8,119.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear and logistic regression models on ABCD Year 2-Year 3 prospective cohort data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in American Journal of Preventive Medicine; no duplicate flag.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) score as IV has own regression coefficients paired with individual-trait outcomes (e.g., depressive problems β=0.42; AOR for suicidal behaviors=1.86). MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) similarly paired with mental health/sleep/substance outcomes (β=0.31 depressive problems; AOR suicide=1.57). Both are qualifying phone/SM-specific variables; other side is individual-trait-level (psychiatric symptoms, suicidal behaviors, sleep disturbance, substance use initiation).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple β coefficients and adjusted odds ratios (AORs) with 95% CIs reported in Figures 1 and 2 and Appendix Table 3 for SMAQ, MPIQ, and VGAQ associations with all outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as FT-IC3; numeric results reported throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media (SMAQ) and mobile phone (MPIQ) are IVs predicting mental health and substance use outcomes (DVs); EC7 requires SM to be DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere, which is not the case here.
notes: VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire) also included as a third exposure. Paper qualifies via SMAQ and MPIQ alone regardless of VGAQ. Longitudinal design (Year 2 exposure, Year 3 outcome).

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes U.S. ABCD Study (5.1 release), baseline 2016-2018 to Year 2 2018-2020, 21 sites, N=7528.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: group-based trajectory modeling and linear regression on ABCD prospective cohort data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Lancet Regional Health Americas; no duplicate flag.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media time trajectory (hours/day on social media specifically, e.g., Instagram, Facebook, Twitter) is the IV with own regression coefficients paired with cognitive outcomes (RAVLT, LMT). E.g., low increasing SM trajectory: RAVLT Initial Trials B=-1.38 (95% CI: -1.82, -0.94); high increasing SM: LMT accuracy B=-0.03 (95% CI: -0.05, -0.01). Social media is a qualifying phone/SM-specific variable; cognitive performance (verbal memory, visuospatial attention) is individual-trait-level.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 2 reports β coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for all social media trajectory group comparisons across four cognitive outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as FT-IC3; numeric results fully reported in Table 2.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media time is the IV (exposure) and cognitive performance is the DV (outcome); EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere, which is not the case here.
notes: Non-social media screen time (TV, video games, texting, video chat) included as a covariate only, not as the main exposure. Social media time is measured separately and specifically as the qualifying IV.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes U.S. ABCD Study (release 5.0), children aged 9-11 at 21 U.S. sites, data from 2016-2019.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear regression and Gsens genetic sensitivity analysis using ABCD cohort data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open; no duplicate flag.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No numeric estimate where the variable label is phone/SM-specific. The primary exposure is total child-reported daily screen time (14-item Screen Time Questionnaire). No separate phone-specific or SM-specific coefficient is reported anywhere in the main paper or supplement. Step 2: The composite screen time measure includes TV/video watching and video games (sensitivity analyses explicitly decompose it into 'watching television or videos' and 'playing video games' as the two most common subtypes; eFigure 4 in Supplement 1). The composite is NOT exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items; it bundles TV and console/video games without a phone-only or SM-only breakout with its own coefficient paired with an outcome. NOT_MET under Step 2 (non-phone/non-SM ingredients bundled in without separate breakout).

notes: The Gsens model estimates genetic confounding in the screen time-psychiatric problems association. The two sensitivity subtypes examined are TV/videos and video games, neither of which is a phone/SM-specific variable. No social media or phone-specific estimate is reported anywhere in the paper.
